speaker: interviewer
text: Per Anger. When you came back to Stockholm, at what time did you learn that Raoul Wallenberg was disappeared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we-- it was a-- a question you asked yourself. How come that the Soviets have told us, they communicated, on the 16th of January, from 1945, with the Swedish embassy in Moscow, telling them that, we have found Raoul Wallenberg, and he is under our protection?

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Stockholm, they were very happy to get that message. But they didn't know anything about us. They thought that we were long dead or-- or whatever. They sort of tried to concentrate their effort to find out what happened to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in a way, you can say that you lost much time there in-- in doing that and not inquire more about Raoul Wallenberg. So not till we came home to Sweden, then they understood that something has happened here.

speaker: interviewer
text: He is-- he is not with you here? And what-- what has happened to him? And then I learned, of course, the same time, as this, that. I was asked then, what I-- what did I think about this? Having just heard about the-- the deportation of the Swiss diplomats and all that, I said to myself, and I said to-- to the people in the Foreign Office or the government, that I believe-- I strongly believe that Raoul Wallenberg has been taken by the Soviets. I couldn't prove it, of course. And then, no, no. How could you say things like that? The Soviets, they have been wonderful people. You know they have helped the whole world to defeat the Nazis and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not only Sweden who said that. It was the whole world. The whole Western world was very grateful to the Soviets for-- for having contributed to this, to the-- to this end of the war. Not till 1948, when the-- after the Prague situation and all that, did the eyes open for what really Soviet was after and so on and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first time that, after the war, they-- they-- they thought that I was very perhaps naive or or-- and so-- I-- But I was convinced. So I was then transferred to-- to another post, to Cairo, where we-- where we were, my wife and I, two years, like '46 and '48.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they, all of the sudden, called me back to the Foreign Office, and said, no. Now we have heard some about, that he is-- must be-- he's-- he's probably in the Soviet Union somewhere. Now you take over the file here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was-- I got kind of a acknowledgement of my-- what I thought from the beginning. I-- I worked with this for two years, between '48 and '50. And I saw that the only way here, the only language the-- the Soviets will understand would be that you speak their language, that you sort of suggest a-- an exchange.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had spies, Russian spies, in Sweden, at that time, coming and going. So as soon as they were discovered, they were sent-- expelled to-- to the Soviet Union. And we never kept any of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told the Foreign Office, the foreign minister, at that time, that my suggestion, sir, is that we-- we-- we see to it that we keep some spies here, now, and make an exchange with-- with the-- with the Soviets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his-- he flatly answered that we, the Swedish government, doesn't deal with that kind of a-- deal with questions that way. We don't exchange people with-- we-- we don't do that and so on. Which I thought wasn't a very practical way of looking at this, because we have been exchanging prisoners of war and all that during all the time of history. I mean that kind of exchange.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they-- they-- the Swedish government, at that time, refused to use that method. And so I resigned from-- from this job and said, could I-- could you please transfer me to another desk?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was then dealing with other matters in the Foreign Office. And I've been doing that ever since, till I retired, when I-- when I have engaged myself for the Raoul Wallenberg case, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which year did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: I resigned from that job in the 1950. Now, not-- you could say, perhaps, that you didn't have any definite evidence, definite proof that he was taken and imprisoned in the Soviet Union. But my idea was that that day will come. And in the meantime, please, hold back some of the spies here for future exchange. That was my idea. Now, it didn't last till one-- till the year after, 1951, when the first Italian diplomats came back from prison, in Lubyanka Prison in Moscow, where they had been sitting in the same cell as-- as Raoul Wallenberg, or had been knocking in the wall with him. They have been in communication with Raoul Wallenberg, immediately after the war, in '45, '46, or whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there we had-- we had the witnesses. And so the government started to work on that and so on. Did you went to Soviet Union during that time, between 1948 and '51?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Traveling to Soviet Union, visiting Soviet Union during that time? If you have been visiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah! No, no. No. I-- I've not been on that desk. I have not been dealing with Soviet affairs. So I-- I never visited the Soviet Union, again, till 19-- 1989, when we were invited to come and discuss the Raoul Wallenberg case. So-- so during all these years, you know, when I-- after my retirement in 1980, 15 years, I've been traveling around in the world, working for his case, trying to mobilize foreign governments for-- for his case, and all that. Of course, he's not only-- this is not only a Swedish case, it's the whole world must be concerned about his fate. Because he's become the-- the symbol for the fight for human rights, you know, and so on. Now all of these efforts, from other governments, from the American presidents, and from heads of states, didn't work as long as the communistic rule was there. But as soon as the Perestroika came about and, above all, after the-- the coup, the fate of the coup, 199-- 1991, then it worked. And then the Soviets said-- the Russians said, of course, now we have to do something. We have to do something. And that's when we were invited. That's when they established a commission, a Swedish-Russian commission to look into the documents and so on and so on and so on. That's how it stands today. And they-- they have still-- they have still not found the-- the crucial document, which tells us what really happened. They have not been able to prove his death. Consequently, the Swedish government's view is that, as long as you haven't been able to prove that he's dead, we take it there could be a possibility that he might still be alive somewhere. And our theory would be that they let him, say, in 1947, when we have no more documents about him after '47, that they destroyed the documents. They let him disappear. They-- they thought that this was an embarrassing situation for them to have a Swedish diplomat in jail. And they probably said to themselves, why don't we let him disappear for a while now, under another name. And perhaps later on, we could-- Now, it-- it is too embarrassing to admit now that we have him here. Perhaps, after a couple of years, we could take up the question with the Swedes if we could perhaps get something for him or whatever. That is a theory we believe in. That he-- and he might-- they might have sent him to a mental hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you personally think that Raoul Wallenberg or his remains are today?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think? Where do you think Raoul Wallenberg is today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- that's an impossible question, because there are hundreds of these mental hospitals and all that. But that would confirm the evidence of a Swedish doctor, Schwartz, Nana Schwartz, who went to-- to Moscow in 1961, and spoke to her counterpart, a-- an-- a Russian doctor, and asked him about Wallenberg, when that Russian doctor says, well, he's in a mental hospital here, nearby. The Soviet authorities, this government, denied and said that's absolutely a misunderstanding. You could ask him again, if you want, in our presence. And he was asked in-- in the presence of-- of the Soviet dignitaries. And this doctor said that, I have never heard the name, Raoul Wallenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After telling me all your life story, do you want to tell about any special events that are very important for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Understand, for me, it has been a very-- of course, very frustrating to-- this situation, that we all came home but not Raoul Wallenberg. That's why I also-- now he was very-- my very-- I miss my very good friend. That's why I have been working so hard, for him, the whole time. And I still hope that-- we must know what happened to him. There's no doubt about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- and-- and I am sure that, sooner or later, perhaps very late, kind of, perhaps, next generation-- you can't hide things like that forever. It must come to the surface somewhere. I mean, there must be copies of documents. There must be witnesses. And there must be-- you know? So I'm-- I'm-- I'm quite sure that, one day, we will know the truth about what they-- what they did to him. Do you have any message for the coming generation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: --about all of your life and your stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message would be that you-- you should never forget what happened during that-- that time, that terrible time. Because if you forget it, or if you don't learn about it, it's a-- it's a-- the risk will be greater that, bigger that you-- that it will be repeated again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- so the-- the way is here to, first of all, to spread the knowledge about what happened, this-- this Holocaust situation. It-- it should be documented in all kinds of ways, by films and books and so on, interviews like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you should also remember the deeds of Raoul Wallenberg, because that, in a way, gives hope, that, as long as there is a Raoul Wallenberg, there could be another Raoul Wallenberg, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hope for that, and if-- if-- if-- if it would come to the worst again. I mean, as long as a Raoul Wallenberg has existed and done what he did, I think that-- that gives hope. Ambassador Per Anger, thank you very much for this interview. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: English, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, I can say thank you on the film, on the television. I wanted to thank you so many times. And-- and now I have the possibility to thank you and the Swedish rescue mission, and the Swedish legation and Attache Anger. As my mother always wanted to say, Attache Anger. I had a meeting with Attache Anger. So I wanted to thank you, because you were working on a protective passport for us. And we have been able to survive. Thank you for you. Thank you for Raoul. Thank you for the whole Swedish mission, who saved our life. And as a symbol, I wanted to thank you that you stayed behind, in spite of the fact that you could have been killed any moment. And I know, from your wife, she was-- you sent her back with your newborn baby, in October '44-- because Birgitta was born in May or June '44-- with a newborn baby. And your wife didn't know where you are, how you are, whether you survived, you are alive. And I remember that, especially, the Hungarian Nazis, they didn't care, sometimes. They just killed anyone whom they didn't like. So that you risked your life every time you went out to save people. And also, when you have given all these protective documents to thousand people, whom I believe that, if they would have the possibility to come to you today, they would be more than happy--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --and-- and to thank you, what you have done. I can just say recently, Ambassador Anger has been given the B'nai B'rith acknowledgment, in Budapest, for the achievement, what you have done for the Jews, and what you have done to help Raoul, to save also all the people, we are in life, in Budapest. And the lady who was sitting next to me, she was crying, because she said, my grandparents, they were saved. And they have been living in a Swedish protective house. And they had the Swedish protective passport. And I can tell you that it has been, for us, who have been in life, especially now that we know what had happened, that we could survive. I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Could you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is-- in Hungarian, I was born Kati Kadelburger. And now my name is Kate Wacsz, as married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is me standing at the-- the Danube, looking out on the water, in the summer of-- well, it was spring of 19-- 1940-- 43. It was still the time when you still didn't notice very much of the war. It was a calm and a-- quite a nice period. It was like in-- in peacetime really. Standing with-- with our little dog, which we received, my wife and I, as a wedding present from a Hungarian Jewish friend. This is me standing in the front of the embassy building, on the Buda side, in the fall of 1943, together with Professor Langlet. He was the cultural attache at the embassy and, at the same, time, the Red-- Swedish Red Cross delegate. And he did quite-- --he contributed, in a wonderful way, also, to the salvation of the Jewish people in Budapest. This is me standing on the terrace of the embassy building. I think it was at the beginning of 1944. And this is a, so called, provisional passport. I-- just after the-- the occupation, the German occupation, in '44, in March '44, I gave those passports to a handful of people, who had a special relationship with Sweden, who were married to a Swedish citizen, who had-- or heading big Swedish companies in Budapest. Like I signed the provisional passports, I later on then also issued and signed this kind of a document, with stamps on it and so on. It's certifying that the holder of this paper has got a visa to Sweden and was under the Swedish protection. And that helped a lot. We-- we managed, the embassy managed, to save about a hundred people that way. Based on the earlier documents, which the embassy had issued to save Jewish people, Raoul Wallenberg invented this Shutz-Pass. This is a protective passport, which was going to be the salvation for thousands and thousands of people. This is a document proving that I have been appointed 'Righteous Gentile,' and that a-- a tree, for me, was going to be planted in the Avenue of the Righteous of the Yad Vashem in Jerusalem. I'm standing, here, there in the-- in front of the old embassy building in Budapest. And-- and the Hungarians, in 1983, put up a plaque on the wall of the embassy building, in their gratitude for what the Swedish embassy did during the war to save the Jewish people. And in relief, one can see the-- my chief, the Ambassador Danielsson, Raoul Wallenberg, and myself. In November of 1995, I was honored by the Hungarian government in Budapest, through the Hungarian president, Mr. Arpad Goncz, who presented me with a very distinguished Hungarian order for my work during the war and for my efforts for the-- the case of Raoul Wallenberg. To the left, you see the book I wrote about Raoul Wallenberg. There's a Swedish edition appeared in 1979. This is the second edition. It appeared in '85. This, you see to the left. In the middle is the English translation, which appeared in 19-- 1981 in-- in-- in Los-- in Los-- no, in San Francisco, I think it was. And to the right, the right is a book, which has recently been published in Budapest, that contains the reports, my chief, Danielsson, and I, myself, wrote, and also a few reports from Raoul Wallenberg. We wrote to home and sent home by-- by diplomatic mail to Sweden. They are all now-- have all been-- been released, of course, now. It's so long ago. And they have been collected in Hungary and translated into Hungarian. So that is a book, which is now is for sale in-- in Budapest.
